Oil Prices Slide as Funds Ready for Gulf Exports to Resume

Investors have halved their position in Brent anticipating a pickup exports through the Strait of Hormuz as talks between the United States and Iran intensify and more vessels proceed through the waterway under cover of darkness.

Hedge funds and other money managers sold the equivalent of another 44 million barrels of futures and options in the ICE Brent contract over the seven days ending on June 9, according to position records filed with the exchange ...

Recent statements by Iranian and US officials suggest that Iran and the United States are still at odds over the Strait of Hormuz

quotedTweet.text:
NEW | Recent statements by Iranian and US officials suggest that Iran and the United States are still at odds over the Strait of Hormuz. The United States has repeatedly clarified that its position is that Iran cannot control or charge fees or tolls in the Strait of Hormuz. Iranian Foreign Affairs Minister Abbas Araghchi said that Iran will seek to levy fees on traffic in the Strait of Hormuz and administer the strait.

A US official speaking to Reuters said that the deal meets Trump’s “core objectives” and that Iran would “open” the strait in return for the US releasing frozen Iranian assets and waiving sanctions on oil exports.

Trump previously rejected a plan under which Oman and Iran would charge a joint toll for ships passing through the Strait of Hormuz. It is unclear how a toll differs from a fee, and Iranian management of an international waterway would represent a major change in long-established maritime legal precedent.

text: Goldman lowers 2027 Brent oil forecast on supply growth, demand risks | Reuters https://t.co/qdf8COcG18
Goldman lowers 2027 Brent oil forecast on supply growth, demand risks | Reuters https://t.co/rutKzmfLG2
Goldman lowers 2027 Brent oil forecast on supply growth, demand risks | Reuters https://t.co/8ItHbBN6XL

Goldman Sachs lowered its 2027 oil price forecast, expecting Brent crude to average $80/bbl as stronger supply growth and weaker demand—particularly from China’s shift toward EVs—weigh on the market. However, the bank warned prices could exceed $110/bbl if disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz persist.
Goldman lowers 2027 Brent oil forecast on supply growth, demand risks #oott https://t.co/WoZZSfZ3OQ

Oil fell below USD 90 after signs of progress toward a U.S.-Iran deal.

Goldman Sachs lowered its 2027 average Brent forecast to USD 80, citing supply growth and demand risks.

Gold is heading for a second weekly loss, even with geopolitical risk still on the table.

Three different assets, three different signals: oil is pricing de-escalation, Goldman is pricing oversupply, and gold is pricing higher rates over uncertainty.

When safe havens and risk premiums move in the same direction, the market is making a bet on rates, not on peace.
#Oil #Gold #Macro #Brent

Daily Market Report #764 TLDR

It is Saturday. Full World Cup fever here.

I am tipping most games on prediction markets and I am 2 out of 4 so far.

The $SPCX IPO happened yesterday, and it is all the market wants to talk about.

Trading around $160 today.

Pre-IPO perps had it at $180.

US equities added $1.15T in a day, Nasdaq up 3%.

Gold is still soft at $4,180, and the war premium leaving gold and oil tells you the market is de-risking the geopolitical trade.

$BTC is sitting at $63,487, basically flat.

Dominance 56.4%.

Spot BTC ETFs logged a fifth straight outflow day and have bled $2.1B since June started.

The institutional bid is still missing, but Morgan Stanley raised its Bitcoin holdings to 3,850 BTC via spot ETF.

$ETH is at $1,664, flat.

ETH TVL is up again, and 7-day flows are finally green across Ethereum, Solana, Arbitrum, and Base for the first time in over a month.

$SOL is at $66.73, flat, but it had the strongest fundamental week in my universe.

SPCX trading rails.

WSOP sponsorship.

Superteam Germany hosting the largest Solana event ever in Berlin.

Token doing nothing.

Ecosystem shipping hard.

$XRP is at $1.13.

JPMorgan and Ripple are testing 5-second XRP settlement, the kind of real-bank pilot XRP holders waited years for.

$HYPE is at $58.16.

Circle transferred $4B $USDC to Hyperliquid, an enormous liquidity vote even as the token cools.

$XMR is at $344, cooling after a huge week but still the privacy leader.

$ZEC is at $414.

AI is now both the auditor and the attacker, and after the Opus 4.8 audit found the Zcash bug, the security layer clearly has not caught up.

The collector itch is real.

Avalanche Treasury sank 38% on its Nasdaq debut, a reminder that the crypto-treasury-goes-public trade is not a guaranteed win.

NFTfi is shutting down as NFT lending contracts.

Japan passed a crypto bill classifying assets as financial instruments and cutting taxes, a real regulated-market unlock. $ADA is done. 

Bezos’s AI venture Prometheus raised $12B.

ChatGPT hit 1B monthly app users.

And developers are now saying Claude Fable 5 plays it too safe.

After I praised it earlier this week for fixing things I had not thought of, the broader dev consensus is that the safety guardrails went too far.

What are you watching this weekend?

quotedTweet.text:
Daily Market Report #764

It is Saturday. Full World Cup fever here.

I am tipping most of the games on prediction markets, and I am 2 out of 4 so far. Not a disaster, not a brag. Football is humbling in the same way trading is. You can read everything right and still watch a red card in the 12th minute torch your whole thesis. I will keep sharing the slips, wins and losses both.

The SpaceX IPO happened yesterday, and it is the only thing the market wants to talk about. Priced at $135, Elon is officially a trillionaire, and the stock is trading around $160 today after the pre-IPO perps had it at $180.

The detail I want to flag: Solana was the only platform where you could trade SPCX out of the gate yesterday. Not Hyperliquid, not a TradFi broker, Solana. On a day everyone was focused on Hyperliquid’s pre-IPO perps, Solana quietly had the rails working when it counted.

Stocks already ripped, US equities added $1.15T in a day, Nasdaq up 3%.

Gold $4,180, still soft. The war premium leaving gold and oil at the same time tells you this is real de-risking of the geopolitical trade, not fear.

Bitcoin $63,487, basically flat. Dominance 56.4%. Holding the range while everything around it moves. Spot BTC ETFs logged a fifth straight outflow day and have bled $2.1B since June started, with ETF volumes down 78% from peak.

The institutional bid is still missing.

But Morgan Stanley raised its Bitcoin holdings to 3,850 BTC via spot ETF, so it is not everyone leaving. It is rotation and thinning volume.

Saylor repeated his line again: never said Strategy would not sell.

He is going to keep saying it until people stop being angry, which will be never.

Metaplanet bought a Japanese securities firm to build BTC-linked products, the treasury-company-becomes-a-bank playbook continuing.

Ethereum $1,664, flat. ETH TVL up another 0.9%, and 7-day flows are finally green across Ethereum, Solana, Arbitrum, and Base for the first time in over a month.

The bleed has genuinely stopped at the chain level, even if price is flat.

Vitalik teased liquidation-free DeFi on the way, which would be a real structural shift if it ships.

Solana $66.73, flat, but the strongest story in my universe this week on fundamentals.

The SPCX trading rails, the WSOP sponsorship, and Superteam Germany hosting the largest Solana event ever in Berlin.

While the token does nothing, the Solana ecosystem is throwing the biggest parties and shipping the most product.

That gap usually resolves up eventually.

XRP $1.13, down 1%.

JPMorgan and Ripple are testing 5-second XRP settlement, which is the kind of real-bank pilot XRP holders have waited years for.

Sentiment at multi-month lows, usage down 91%, and the banks are quietly testing it anyway.

Hyperliquid $58.16, flat. Circle transferred $4B USDC to Hyperliquid, an enormous liquidity vote even as the token cools.

The Kalshi-perps competition story from yesterday still stands, but $4B in fresh stablecoin liquidity says the platform is not going anywhere.

Beaten token, thriving venue.

Monero $344, down 6.5%. Giving back some of the 18% week, but still the privacy leader.

Zcash $414, down 4.5%.

The privacy bid cools on a flat day, which is normal after a rip.

Immunefi’s CEO called AI the biggest new threat to DeFi smart contract security, which lands hard given the Opus 4.8 audit that found the Zcash bug.

AI is now both the auditor and the attacker, and the security layer has not caught up.

ARB unlock Tuesday and STRK unlock Monday, both worth watching into thin weekend liquidity.

Pengu $0.0067, and the Pudgy Vibes TCG Season 3 window is still open.

Avalanche Treasury sank 38% on its Nasdaq debut, a rough reminder that the crypto-treasury-goes-public trade is not a guaranteed win.

NFTfi is shutting down as the NFT lending market contracts, and that one stings as a collector.

The quiet infrastructure of the last NFT cycle is closing one door at a time.

Japan passed a crypto bill classifying assets as financial instruments and cutting taxes, a genuinely big regulated-market unlock.

SpaceX aside, the numbers stay surreal.

Bezos’s AI venture Prometheus raised $12B.

ChatGPT hit 1B monthly app users.

And the line that matters most: developers are now saying Claude Fable 5 plays it too safe.

After I praised it earlier this week for fixing things I had not thought of, the broader dev consensus is that the safety guardrails went too far.

Anthropic walked back the sabotage policy, and the market is telling them the model is too cautious. It’s crazy also that US stoped Fable.

Fascinating problem to have, building something so capable that the debate is whether it is too careful.

What are you watching this weekend?

text:
🟢 $QQQ $721.34 (+0.59%) (Fri close)
— A senior US official says the agreement with Iran includes opening the Strait of Hormuz as a fundamental condition, with the passage expected to reopen free of transit fees. This is a significant development for global oil flows, as the Strait handles roughly a fifth of the world's seaborne crude. WTI crude fell 3.23% on the news, reflecting expectations of restored supply through one of the world's most critical chokepoints.

🇺🇲 Trump on Truth Social this morning - the clearest statement yet: "The Deal is scheduled to get signed tomorrow. Immediately after it is signed, the Strait of Hormuz is OPEN TO ALL."

"They no longer want a nuclear weapon, nor will they have one - through purchase, development, or any other form of procurement."

"No money will exchange hands. At the appropriate time, we will go in and get the Nuclear Dust - buried deep under the sunken granite mountains - and downblend and destroy it."

Pakistan's PM Sharif this morning: finalization "likely expected in the next 24 hours." Pakistan is "preparing for the electronic signing immediately after."

Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesman Baghaei, hours later: "It will not be tomorrow."

Brent crude: $84/barrel. Down from $98 at the peak. The market is pricing the signing before both sides confirm it.

The deal is scheduled for tomorrow. Iran says not tomorrow. Trump says tomorrow. The Pakistan mediator says tomorrow.

One of them is right.

⚠️ 🇺🇸 🇮🇷 Tehran and Washington on the Verge of Finalizing an Agreement, Says Pakistani Mediator

Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif announced that the United States and Iran have reached a final text for a peace deal aimed at ending their conflict in the Middle East, a statement met with cautious optimism in Tehran.

Pakistan has been acting as the main intermediary in indirect talks between Washington and Tehran for several months. On Friday, Mr. Sharif stated that “a definitive and mutually agreed text” for a peace agreement had been achieved, and that his country is working closely with both sides to prepare the next steps, including a possible electronic signing in the coming hours or days, followed by technical discussions next week.

The emerging deal is expected to cover the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz — a vital chokepoint for much of the world’s oil — and the lifting of the U.S. naval blockade on Iranian ports. More in-depth negotiations on Iran’s nuclear program are to follow over a roughly 60-day period. Questions remain, however, about the exact scope of Iranian commitments regarding its nuclear and ballistic capabilities, as well as the extent of sanctions relief and the unfreezing of assets.

The conflict, which erupted in late February 2026 with American-Israeli strikes on targets in Iran, has caused thousands of casualties and severely disrupted global energy supplies after Tehran closed the strait. A temporary ceasefire was reached in April under the same Pakistani mediation, but the current talks aim for a more lasting resolution.

Tehran responded with restraint. The spokesman for Iran’s Foreign Ministry said confirmation of the exact signing date for a memorandum of understanding is still pending, noting it would not happen the next day and that vigilance remains necessary due to hesitation from the other side.

On X, reactions from the American right often reflect cautious skepticism about the terms of any potential deal, raising questions about its ability to durably protect U.S. security interests and prevent a resurgence of Iranian nuclear ambitions. On the left, comments tend to emphasize the human cost of the conflict and the need for a verifiable de-escalation, while acknowledging Pakistan’s active diplomatic role in a particularly volatile regional file.

China's real leverage — Russia and China could give Iran a nuclear capability. They have not. Why? Because the Strait of Hormuz is worth more than the nuclear card. A closed Strait collapses the global economy. A nuke in Iran is a negotiating tool. The Strait is an extinction-level event. #China #Iran #Oil #Macro #Geopolitics $OIL

Think about that the next time you hear about nuclear talks.

Understanding this changes everything about the current moment.

过去 2 小时跟踪了全网 66 条新闻消息，筛出 5 条核心信号。The Islamabad MOU marks a tactical truce, but with Cushing inventories at a 21.6 million barrel razor's edge and Iran eyeing 'service fees' for the Strait of Hormuz, the cost of physical energy delivery is structurally rising.

1. US-Iran MOU triggers tactical de-escalation. The Islamabad agreement focuses on ending current hostilities while deferring nuclear and sanction details to later stages. Expect a slow bleed of geopolitical risk premium rather than a sudden collapse as execution remains conditional.
$CVX $XOM

2. Iran pivots to 'service fees' for Hormuz transit. Foreign Minister Araghchi claims charging for passage is unacceptable but service fees are legally permitted, effectively creating a new tax on 20% of global oil. This shifts the bottleneck from military blockade to a permanent structural cost for energy markets.
$XOM $BP

3. Cushing crude inventory hits operational redline. Stocks at the key US hub have dropped to 21.6 million barrels, just 1.6 million above the 20 million barrel threshold required to maintain pipeline pressure. Physical delivery constraints are now a higher probability than paper-driven price movements.
$XOM $CVX

4. Transformer scarcity enters high-margin phase. Leading manufacturers report full order books through 2027 with active price hikes as AI data centers and grid upgrades collide. The bottleneck for AI scaling has moved from GPUs to the heavy electrical equipment required to power them.
$GEV $VRT

5. Cyber instability threatens Iran's financial infrastructure. Major banks report system-wide outages following rumored cyber attacks just as peace talks reach the MOU stage. Financial friction in Tehran could delay the diluting of high-enriched uranium agreed upon in the US-Iran framework.
$PLTR $MSTR

That's the signal from this window. If Cushing hits the 20 million barrel floor next week, will US-Iran peace even matter for WTI? Repost if useful.

The SPR (Strategic Petroleum Reserve) has bought time, not barrels for refiners. US commercial crude inventories drew more than 10 million barrels in the latest EIA week — one of the sharpest weekly draws on record — on top of six prior consecutive weeks of above-estimate declines. Diplomatic resolution doesn't flip that overnight; tanker re-routing back through Hormuz adds weeks of physical lag. The physical market is tighter now than it was before the SPR started releasing.
